revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:阿蘇ピンク石]]


[[File:Higashinorikura Kofun, genshitsu-1-1.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Higashinorikura Kofun|ja|東乗鞍古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashinorikura Kofun|qq=}}'s stone chamber and sarcophagus made of {{ill|Aso Pink Stone|ja|阿蘇ピンク石|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aso Pink Stone|qq=}}]]
'''Aso Pink Stone''' (阿蘇ピンク石) is a type of {{ill|Ignimbrite|en|Ignimbrite|qid=Q256438|lt=Ignimbrite|1=Ignimbrite}} produced in the {{ill|Makado area|ja|馬門地区|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makado area|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} of {{ill|Amizu Town|ja|網津町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amizu Town|1=Amitsumachi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11607196}}, {{ill|Uto, Kumamoto|en|Uto, Kumamoto|qid=Q861262|lt=Uto City|1=Uto, Kumamoto}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|en|Kumamoto Prefecture|qid=Q130308|lt=Kumamoto Prefecture|1=Kumamoto Prefecture}}.
It is also referred to as '''Makado Stone''' (馬門石) after its place of origin{{sfn|Uto City History Compilation Committee|2007|p=556|qq=}}.
Known for its beautiful pink coloration and excellent workability, it was used for {{ill|sarcophagi|ja|石棺|1=Sekkan|1=Sekkan (Sarcophagus)|lt=Sarcophagus|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/sarcophagi|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekkan|qq=}} during the {{ill|Kofun period|en|Kofun period|qid=Q459939|lt=Kofun period|1=Kofun period}} and later for {{ill|Ishibashi|en|Ishibashi|qid=Q19839143|lt=stone bridges|1=Ishibashi}} and {{ill|Piping|en|Piping|qid=Q3679502|lt=water pipes|1=Piping}} in the modern era{{sfn|Uto City History Compilation Committee|2007|p=556|qq=}}.
== Regarding the Name ==
As the name suggests, Aso Welded Tuff is formed by {{ill|Pyroclastic flow|en|Pyroclastic flow|qid=Q221616|lt=pyroclastic flows|1=Pyroclastic flow}} from {{ill|Mount Aso|en|Mount Aso|qid=Q733710|lt=Mount Aso|1=Mount Aso}}{{sfn|Takahito Fujimoto|2007|p=1-2|qq=}}, and it is distributed widely across central Kyushu, reaching as far as {{ill|Ube, Yamaguchi|en|Ube, Yamaguchi|qid=Q468765|lt=Ube|1=Ube, Yamaguchi}} in {{ill|Yamaguchi Prefecture|en|Yamaguchi Prefecture|qid=Q127264|lt=Yamaguchi Prefecture|1=Yamaguchi Prefecture}} across the {{ill|Suo Sea|ja|周防灘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suo Sea|1=Suō Sea|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11416963}}{{sfn|Yukihisa Kudo, Goro Hori|2007|p=45-47|qq=}}. Aso Welded Tuff is also known as '''Aso Stone''', a term used in archaeological studies of Kofun period sarcophagi. Modern {{ill|stonemasons|ja|石工|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/stonemasons|1=Stonemason|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q328325}} refer to it based on its properties as '''Hai Stone''' (灰石) or by its place of origin. The Makado area of Amizu Town produces what is referred to as Makado Stone{{sfn|Tomoo Ikeda|2007|p=13-14|qq=}}.
While Aso Stone is generally grayish-black, Makado Stone sometimes appears in pink, beige, or brown. Among these, the pink type is specifically called Aso Pink Stone, although this is also an archaeological term. Locally, Aso Pink Stone is commonly called '''Red Stone''' (赤石) or narrowly as Makado Stone{{sfn|Tomoo Ikeda|2007|p=13-14|qq=}}{{sfn|Takahito Fujimoto|2007|p=12|qq=}}.
This article distinguishes all stones from the Makado area as Makado Stone and refers to the pink-colored variety as Aso Pink Stone.
== Origin and Characteristics ==
Makado Stone was formed by the cooling and solidification of pyroclastic flows from a Mount Aso eruption (Aso-4) approximately 90,000 years ago{{sfn|Uto City History Compilation Committee|2007|p=556|qq=}}. It is thought that the weakly welded upper layers of tens-of-meters-thick pyroclastic flow deposits formed it. Due to the presence of {{ill|Columnar jointing|en|Columnar jointing|qid=Q15427433|lt=columnar joints|1=Columnar jointing}} with wide spacing and few internal cracks, it likely took a long time to cool{{sfn|Yukihisa Kudo, Goro Hori|2007|p=45-47|qq=}}. Although welded tuff is generally dark brown or gray, Makado Stone includes pink and beige varieties. The pink hue deepens when wet. In upper layers of the stone bed, the pink coloration fades and porosity increases{{sfn|Yukihisa Kudo, Goro Hori|2007|p=45-47|qq=}}.
The pink coloration is thought to result from iron oxidation{{sfn|Owji Itabashi|2007|p=20-27|qq=}}, with specific factors during deposition and welding contributing, although these conditions are not yet identified{{sfn|Takahito Fujimoto|2007|p=2-3|qq=}}.
Welded tuff often contains lens-shaped obsidian, but Makado Stone has little of it, as well as smaller and fewer pumices{{sfn|Yukihisa Kudo, Goro Hori|2007|p=45-47|qq=}}. It contains inclusions such as {{ill|Plagioclase|en|Plagioclase|qid=Q714830|lt=plagioclase|1=Plagioclase}}, {{ill|Pyroxene|en|Pyroxene|qid=Q192880|lt=pyroxene|1=Pyroxene}}, and {{ill|Amphibole|en|Amphibole|qid=Q17159|lt=amphibole|1=Amphibole}}{{sfn|Uto City History Compilation Committee|2007|p=556|qq=}}. Classified as soft stone, it is highly workable and suitable for carving reliefs and incising{{sfn|Tomoo Ikeda|2007|p=14-22|qq=}}.
== Usage and History ==
{{External media|image1=[https://www.city.uto.lg.jp/museum/article/view/46/297.html Semba Bridge (Uto City Digital Museum)]||image2=[https://www.city.uto.lg.jp/museum/article/view/46/295.html Torok Water Source and Pipes (Uto City Digital Museum)]|qq=}}
The oldest known use of Makado Stone, as of 2007, is as stone partitions in the {{ill|Nagasaren Kofun|ja|長砂連古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagasaren Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Kami-Amakusa|en|Kami-Amakusa|qid=Q877232|lt=Kamiamakusa|1=Kami-Amakusa}}, Kumamoto Prefecture, dating to the early 5th century{{sfn|Takahito Fujimoto|2007|p=1-2|qq=}}. Usage extended until the mid-6th century in the Uto Peninsula area for stone chamber-type horizontal Kofun walls and passageways{{sfn|Takahito Fujimoto|2007|p=3-7|qq=}}. By the mid-5th century, boat-shaped sarcophagi made of Makado Stone appeared and spread to the {{ill|Kansai region|en|Kansai region|qid=Q164256|lt=Kinki region|1=Kansai region}} via the Chugoku region by the late 5th century, continuing to be used for elite burials into the early 6th century. During the Kofun period, the {{ill|Yamato Kingship|en|Yamato Kingship|qid=Q2658252|lt=Yamato Court|1=Yamato Kingship}} is thought to have monopolized Aso Pink Stone{{sfn|Koji Takagi, Takahito Fujimoto|2009|p=26-29|qq=}}.
The {{ill|Kumano Gongen Worship Stone|ja|熊野権現礼拝石|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Gongen Worship Stone|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} at [[Shitennō-ji]] is made of Aso Pink Stone. While it was installed after the late Heian period based on excavation findings, the material may have been reused{{sfn|Owji Itabashi|2007|p=160-163|qq=}}.
In later eras, Makado Stone was used for {{ill|Gorintō|en|Gorintō|qid=Q304906|lt=Gorintō|1=Gorintō}}, {{ill|Hōkyōintō|en|Hōkyōintō|qid=Q3146365|lt=Hōkyōintō|1=Hōkyōintō}}, and other stone pagodas, while during the modern era, its applications expanded to water pipes, {{ill|Torii|en|Torii|qid=Q234731|lt=torii|1=Torii}}, and {{ill|hand-washing basins|ja|手水舎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hand-washing basins|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 手水|1=Chōzuya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1069667}}. During the late Edo period, it was designated as a government-use stone with specific officials overseeing its management{{sfn|Koji Takagi, Takahito Fujimoto|2009|p=26-29|qq=}}.
In the 1950s, concrete replaced Makado Stone in most uses{{sfn|Uto City History Compilation Committee|2009|p=721-722|qq=}}, but some structures remain in use today, such as water pipes and stone bridges{{sfn|Uto City History Compilation Committee|2007|p=556|qq=}}.
== Makado Ishi Quarry Site ==
[[File:Quarry Site of Makado Ishi.jpg|thumb|250px|right|Makado Ishi Quarry Site]]
{{External media|image1=[https://www.city.uto.lg.jp/museum/article/view/46/319.html Makado Ishi Quarry Site (Uto City Digital Museum)]|qq=}}
In the central part of the {{ill|Uto Peninsula|en|Uto Peninsula|qid=Q11449190|lt=Uto Peninsula|1=Uto Peninsula}}, there is a mountain range stretching east to west. On the northern side, within the valley along the Amitsu River, lies the Makado Ishi Quarry Site covering an area of 1.1 km east-west and 1.5 km north-south. Three quarry remains have been identified here, named "Makado Quarry Site," "Nozoe Quarry Site," and "Kiyotsuji Quarry Site." Among these, the largest and most seaward is the Makado Quarry Site, where {{ill|Aso Pink Stone|ja|阿蘇ピンク石|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aso Pink Stone|qq=}} is most widely distributed.{{sfn|Fujimoto Takahito|2007|p=3-7|qq=}}{{sfn|Takagi Kyoji, Fujimoto Takahito|2009|p=26-29|qq=}}
As noted in the {{ill|Uses and History|ja|用途と歴史|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uses and History|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, during the Kofun period, Aso Pink Stone was used exclusively in the Kinki region, suggesting that Makado Quarry was a special quarry for the Yamato court, while stones from other quarries were used locally. At the center of Makado Quarry is a small hill called Doi-yama, topped with Akaishi Shrine. The current quarry remains date back to the Edo period, using wedge hole techniques, but during the Kofun period, large boulders were likely used.{{sfn|Fujimoto Takahito|2007|p=3-7|qq=}}
Excavations since 2002 uncovered stone debris believed to be from the stone-processing period, along with fragments of Haji pottery and Sue pottery dating from the mid-5th to early 7th centuries. Additionally, medieval celadon and white porcelain, and early modern ceramics were unearthed, clarifying the periods during which Makado Ishi was utilized.{{sfn|Takagi Kyoji, Fujimoto Takahito|2009|p=26-29|qq=}} The stone debris associated with Kofun period pottery suggests rough shaping of sarcophagi was performed at the quarry.{{sfn|Ikeda Tomoo|2007|p=23-24|qq=}}
<gallery>
File:Maki-jinja (Uto), torii.jpg|Maki Shrine near the quarry site (Torii made of Makado Ishi)
</gallery>
== Aso Pink Stone Sarcophagi ==
Research on tuff house-shaped sarcophagi in the Kinki region began with {{ill|Wada Seigo|ja|和田晴吾|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wada Seigo|1=Seigo Wada|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11418030}}. In 1976, Wada noted that some sarcophagi were made of Aso stone, initially believed to be from {{ill|Mount Nijō (Nara/Osaka)|ja|二上山|1=Futakami Mountain|2=ja|3=二上山|lt=Mount Futakami (or Nijo)|comment=Kanji are ambiguous between the two pronounciations|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Nijō (Nara/Osaka)|13=User:Immanuelle/Futakami Mountain|qq=}} and referred to as Nijōzan Pink Tuff. However, in 1985, {{ill|Takagi Kyoji|ja|高木恭二|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takagi Kyoji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} discovered that sarcophagi in the Yanboshi Kofun in Uto City were made of Makado Ishi.{{sfn|Ota Hiroaki|2007|p=46-47|qq=}} Prior to this discovery, Makado Ishi was thought to have been used only since the early modern period.{{sfn|Itabashi Oji|2007|p=27-31|qq=}}
In 1990, Takagi Kyoji and {{ill|Watanabe Kazunori|ja|渡辺一徳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Watanabe Kazunori|1=Kazunori Watanabe|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q125399001}} analyzed sarcophagi from {{ill|Tsukiyama Kofun (Okayama)|ja|築山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukiyama Kofun (Okayama)|1=Tsukiyama Kofun (disambiguation)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11603500}} in Okayama Prefecture, identifying them as Makado Ishi (thereafter called Aso Pink Stone), previously believed to be Nijōzan Pink Tuff. Subsequent confirmations were made in 1994 at {{ill|Kabayama Kofun|ja|甲山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kabayama Kofun|1=Kabutoyama Kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q65274002}} and {{ill|Maruyama Kofun (Yasu)|ja|丸山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Maruyama Kofun (Yasu)|qq=}}, in 1999 at {{ill|Imashirozuka Kofun|en|Imashirozuka Kofun|qid=Q11376795|lt=Imashirozuka Kofun|1=Imashirozuka Kofun}}, and in 2000 at {{ill|Ueyama Kofun|en|Ueyama Kofun|qid=Q11540500|lt=Ueyama Kofun|1=Ueyama Kofun}}. Currently, 13 Aso Pink Stone sarcophagi have been confirmed.{{sfn|Ota Hiroaki|2007|p=46-47|qq=}}
Some theories suggest Makado Ishi sarcophagi predate those made from Nijōzan White Stone and Tatsuyama Stone. However, accompanying artifacts indicate all three materials were used around the same period.{{sfn|Ota Hiroaki|2007|p=47-49|qq=}}
While sarcophagi made from the three stones exhibit distinct features, suggesting different origins, they eventually influenced each other, leading to integrated styles.{{sfn|Ota Hiroaki|2007|p=49-51|qq=}} The protrusion shapes and sizes of Makado Ishi sarcophagi lids suggest origins in the Kyushu region.{{sfn|Ota Hiroaki|2007|p=49-51|qq=}}
Takagi posits that the sarcophagus in {{ill|Kamokago Kofun|ja|鴨籠古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamokago Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in Uto City served as the prototype for that in Okayama's Tsukiyama Kofun (both made from Makado Ishi), linked through the fire (肥) clan and the {{ill|Kibi clan|en|Kibi clan|qid=Q11412645|lt=Kibi Clan|1=Kibi clan}}.{{sfn|Takahama Hidetoshi|2007|p=115-118|qq=}}
Conversely, {{ill|Itakunasu Kazuko|ja|板楠和子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Itakunasu Kazuko|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} suggests the {{ill|Nukadabe Clan|ja|額田部氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nukadabe Clan|1=Nukata clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q55532736}} controlled river transport along the {{ill|Yamato River|en|Yamato River|qid=Q9001527|lt=Yamato River|1=Yamato River}} during the Kofun period, possibly involved in transporting Aso Pink Stone sarcophagi from Uto Peninsula to Naniwatsu via the Seto Inland Sea.{{sfn|Itakunasu Kazuko|2007|p=181-188|qq=}}
In the late 6th century, Aso Pink Stone sarcophagi briefly disappeared, only to reappear at Ueyama Kofun. This revival may have been linked to the relationship between the Nukadabe Clan and {{ill|Empress Suiko|en|Empress Suiko|qid=Q298057|lt=Empress Suiko|1=Empress Suiko}}, with special arrangements made for {{ill|Prince Takeda|ja|竹田皇子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Takeda|1=Prince Takeda|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11599359}}.{{sfn|Itakunasu Kazuko|2007|p=184-185|qq=}}
== Stone Coffins Made from Aso Pink Stone ==
Takagi discovered fragments of Aso pink stone from the mound and inside the stone chamber of {{ill|Ueyama Kofun|en|Ueyama Kofun|qid=Q11540500|lt=Ueyama Kofun|1=Ueyama Kofun}}, along with the fact that the lids and bodies of early Sarcophaguss featured an inro-style join not seen in Kyushu. This led him to suggest that the rough finishing was done in Uto before transportation to Kinki, where the final finishing occurred either around the mound or inside the stone chamber{{sfn|Takagi Kyoji|2007|p=142-149|qq=}}{{sfn|Takagi Kyoji|2007|p=149-152|qq=}}.
{| class="wikitable"
|+ Stone Coffins Made from Makado Stone Outside Kyushu{{sfn|Itabashi Oji|2007|p=52|qq=}}

Some argue it originated from Shinjo Kamigurazuka. The lid features linear carvings known as "chokomon"{{sfn|Hayashida Kazuto|2007|p=237-260|qq=}}.
|-
! {{ill|Nagamochiyama Kofun|ja|長持山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagamochiyama Kofun|qq=}}

|-
! {{ill|Minegazuka Kofun|ja|峯ヶ塚古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minegazuka Kofun|qq=}}

Potentially interred {{ill|Emperor Ninken|en|Emperor Ninken|qid=Q357266|lt=Emperor Ninken|1=Emperor Ninken}}?{{sfn|Takagi Kyoji, Fujimoto Takahito|2009|p=26-29|qq=}}
|-
! Bessho Kanzuka Kofun
| Nara Prefecture{{ill|Tenri, Nara|en|Tenri, Nara|qid=Q502196|lt=Tenri|1=Tenri, Nara}} ||  || Boat-shaped Coffin || Unknown || Unknown || First Kofun at the rear. Pre-war investigations confirmed a pink coffin, suggesting Aso pink stone{{sfn|Hayashida Kazuto|2007|p=237-260|qq=}}.
|-

| {{ill|Nara Prefecture|en|Nara Prefecture|qid=Q131287|lt=Nara Prefecture|1=Nara Prefecture}}{{ill|Nara (city)|en|Nara (city)|qid=Q169134|lt=Nara City|1=Nara (city)}} || 4th quarter of the 5th century || Boat-shaped Coffin || Pink || Pink ||

Possible interment of {{ill|Omi-no-Keno|ja|近江毛野|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Omi-no-Keno|1=Oumi no Kena|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q8011171}}?{{sfn|Momozaki Yusuke|2007|p=162-163|qq=}}
|-
! {{ill|Kabutoyama Kofun (Yasu)|ja|甲山古墳 (野洲市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kabutoyama Kofun (Yasu)|qq=}}
| Shiga Prefecture, Yasu City || 2nd quarter of the 6th century || House-shaped Coffin || Pink || Pink ||
|-
! {{ill|Imashirozuka Kofun|en|Imashirozuka Kofun|qid=Q11376795|lt=Imajōzuka Kofun|1=Imashirozuka Kofun}}
| Osaka Prefecture{{ill|Takatsuki|en|Takatsuki|qid=Q335423|lt=Takatsuki|1=Takatsuki}} ||  || House-shaped Coffin || Pink || Pink || Coffin fragments with inro-style joints and red pigments. One of three coffins{{sfn|Hayashida Kazuto|2007|p=237-260|qq=}}. Possible interment of {{ill|Emperor Keitai|en|Emperor Keitai|qid=Q349277|lt=Emperor Keitai|1=Emperor Keitai}}?{{sfn|Takagi Kyoji, Fujimoto Takahito|2009|p=26-29|qq=}}
|-
! {{ill|Higashinorikura Kofun|ja|東乗鞍古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashinorikura Kofun|qq=}}
| Nara Prefecture, Tenri City || 1st quarter of the 6th century || House-shaped Coffin || Pink || Pink || One of two coffins{{sfn|Hayashida Kazuto|2007|p=237-260|qq=}}. Possible interment of {{ill|Mononobe clan|en|Mononobe clan|qid=Q1755836|lt=Mononobe Clan|1=Mononobe clan}} or {{ill|Hozumi Clan|ja|穂積氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hozumi Clan|1=Hozumi clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11597079}}?{{sfn|Momozaki Yusuke|2007|p=163-164|qq=}}
|-
! {{ill|Ueyama Kofun|en|Ueyama Kofun|qid=Q11540500|lt=Ueyama Kofun|1=Ueyama Kofun}}
| Nara Prefecture{{ill|Kashihara, Nara|en|Kashihara, Nara|qid=Q496319|lt=Kashihara|1=Kashihara, Nara}} || 3rd-4th quarter of the 6th century || House-shaped Coffin || Pink || Pink || Excavated from the eastern chamber. No coffin found in the western chamber, but Aso pink stone fragments detected{{sfn|Hayashida Kazuto|2007|p=237-260|qq=}}. Possible interment of {{ill|Prince Takeda|ja|竹田皇子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Takeda|1=Prince Takeda|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11599359}}?{{sfn|Takagi Kyoji, Fujimoto Takahito|2009|p=26-29|qq=}}
|}
<gallery>
File:Osafune Tsukiyama kofun 06.jpg|Stone Coffin of Tsukiyama Kofun
File:Nagamochiyama-kofun sekikan.jpg|Stone Coffin of Nagamochiyama Kofun (right: 2nd coffin)
File:Mirokudani-sekkan.jpg|Mirokudani Stone Coffin
File:今城塚古墳 石棺片.JPG|Presumed Stone Coffin Fragment of Imajōzuka Kofun
File:Higashinorikura Kofun, sekkan-1-1.jpg|Stone Coffin of Higashinorikura Kofun
File:Kabutozuka Kofun (Sakurai), sekkan.jpg|Stone Coffin of Kabutozuka Kofun
File:Maruyama Kofun (Yasu), genshitsu.jpg|Stone Coffin of Maruyama Kofun
File:Kabutoyama Kofun (Yasu), genshitsu.jpg|Tomb of Kabutoyama Kofun
</gallery>
== Daio Coffin Experimental Voyage ==
[[File:西都原古墳群出土 埴輪 船.JPG|thumb|right|{{center|Boat-shaped Haniwa from the Saitobaru Kofun Cluster|qq=}}{{small|(Tokyo National Museum)|qq=}}]]
The Stone Coffin Culture Research Association spearheaded the reproduction and transportation experiment of Aso pink Sarcophaguss. After the preparatory committee was established, the "Daio Coffin Experimental Voyage Executive Committee," consisting of researchers in {{ill|Archaeology|en|Archaeology|qid=Q23498|lt=archaeology|1=Archaeology}}, maritime history, and {{ill|ancient history|ja|古代史|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ancient history|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, was launched in April 2004{{sfn|Uto City History Compilation Committee|2009|p=721-722|qq=}}. The reproduced items included the Sarcophagus, {{ill|shura (sled)|ja|修羅 (そり)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shura (sled)|qq=}}, the barge carrying the coffin, and the ancient ship towing the barge{{sfn|Sugimura Shoichi|2007|p=125-127|qq=}}.
The Sarcophagus was modeled after that of {{ill|Imashirozuka Kofun|en|Imashirozuka Kofun|qid=Q11376795|lt=Imajōzuka Kofun|1=Imashirozuka Kofun}}, with additional references to coffins from {{ill|Maruyama Kofun (Yasu)|ja|円山古墳 (野洲市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Maruyama Kofun (Yasu)|qq=}} and {{ill|Kabutoyama Kofun (Yasu)|ja|甲山古墳 (野洲市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kabutoyama Kofun (Yasu)|qq=}} due to fragmentary remains{{sfn|Furushiro Fumio|2007|p=25-26|qq=}}. Stone selection began in March 2004, facing difficulties due to the required size, taking about three months to extract the stone{{sfn|Takahama Hidetoshi|2007|p=118-119|qq=}}. The extracted raw stone weighed 20 tons for the body and 15 tons for the lid. Rough finishing took about two months, and a sled-pulling experiment was conducted on July 24, 2004{{sfn|Takahama Hidetoshi|2007|p=119-122|qq=}}.
The shura used for land transportation was modeled after one excavated from the {{ill|Mitsuzuka Kofun|ja|三ツ塚古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mitsuzuka Kofun|qq=}} in {{ill|Fujiidera|en|Fujiidera|qid=Q634011|lt=Fujidera, Osaka|1=Fujiidera}}. The wood was sourced from a 250-year-old {{ill|Quercus glauca|en|Quercus glauca|qid=Q714124|lt=Arakashi|1=Quercus glauca}} tree found in {{ill|Uki, Kumamoto|en|Uki, Kumamoto|qid=Q782825|lt=Uki, Kumamoto|1=Uki, Kumamoto}}, with the final dimensions being 6.24 m in length and 1.51 m in maximum width{{sfn|Kimura Hironori, Fujimoto Takahito|2007|p=68-75|qq=}}.
Initially, a catamaran was planned to suspend the coffin in water, but it was deemed unstable. A raft model was then tested, but it sank significantly under the coffin's weight, making towing difficult. Ultimately, separate dugout canoes were made for the body and lid{{sfn|Takagi Kyoji|2007|p=145-148|qq=}}{{sfn|Uno Masatoshi|2007|p=134-135|qq=}}.
The ancient towing ship was modeled after a boat-shaped haniwa excavated from tumulus No. 170 in the {{ill|Saitobaru Kofun Cluster|en|Saitobaru Kofun Cluster|qid=Q3469314|lt=Saitobaru Kofun Cluster|1=Saitobaru Kofun Cluster}} in {{ill|Miyazaki Prefecture|en|Miyazaki Prefecture|qid=Q130300|lt=Miyazaki Prefecture|1=Miyazaki Prefecture}}, supervised by Matsuki Satoshi{{sfn|Takagi Kyoji|2007|p=145-148|qq=}}. Built from two American pine logs, it measured 11.9 m long, 2.1 m wide, and weighed an estimated 6 tons. Named "Kaiō" (Sea King), it had a capacity of 30 people, with 18 rowers on both sides{{sfn|Uto City|2004a|p=7|qq=}}{{sfn|Uto City|2004b|p=13|qq=}}.
The transportation voyage began from Uto City on July 24, 2005{{sfn|Sugimura Shoichi|2007|p=125-127|qq=}}. The route passed through the Ariake Sea, Genkai Sea, Kanmon Strait, and Seto Inland Sea, arriving at {{ill|Osaka Nanko Port|ja|大阪南港|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Osaka Nanko Port|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大阪港|1=Port of Osaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q5354480}} on August 26, covering a total distance of 1006 km{{sfn|Sugimura Shoichi|2007|p=127-129|qq=}}. The experiment proved that coastal support was essential for Sarcophagus transportation, suggesting that the route indicated Yamato regime's control and that coffin transportation may have served as a display of royal authority{{sfn|Takagi Kyoji|2007|p=148-149|qq=}}.
After the experimental voyage, on August 28, a shura-pulling event titled "Daio Stone Coffin Carried by 1,000 People" was held in {{ill|Takatsuki|en|Takatsuki|qid=Q335423|lt=Takatsuki, Osaka|1=Takatsuki}}{{sfn|Kimura Hironori, Fujimoto Takahito|2007|p=73-75|qq=}}.
After the series of transportation experiments, the coffin was finalized. The reproduced lid measured 240 cm in length, 124 cm in width, 65 cm in height, and weighed 2.9 tons. The body was 240 cm long, 125 cm wide, 89 cm high, and weighed 3.8 tons{{sfn|Furushiro Fumio|2007|p=34|qq=}}. The shura used is preserved and displayed at the {{ill|Chikatsu Asuka Museum|ja|近つ飛鳥博物館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chikatsu Asuka Museum|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大阪府立近つ飛鳥博物館|1=Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q4557237}}{{sfn|Kimura Hironori, Fujimoto Takahito|2007|p=73-75|qq=}}.

** {{Cite book|language=ja|author=Uno Masatoshi|title=Ancient Fleet Ports during the Kofun Period|ref={{SfnRef|Uno Masatoshi|2007|qq=}}|qq=}}
** {{Cite book|language=ja|author=Ota Hiroaki|title=Makado Stone House-shaped Coffins Distributed in Kinai Region|ref={{SfnRef|Ota Hiroaki|2007|qq=}}|qq=}}

** {{Cite book|language=ja|author=Sugimura Shoichi|title=Reconstruction of the Stone Coffin Transport Fleet|ref={{SfnRef|Sugimura Shoichi|2007|qq=}}|qq=}}
** {{Cite book|language=ja|author=Takagi Kyoji|title=Kofun Construction Based on Stone Coffin Transport Experiments|ref={{SfnRef|Takagi Kyoji|2007|qq=}}|qq=}}

** {{Cite book|language=ja|author=Hayashida Kazuto|title=Explanation of Aso Stone Coffins Outside Kyushu (Uto Peninsula Origin)|ref={{SfnRef|Hayashida Kazuto|2007|qq=}}|qq=}}
